speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four. Our survivor is Shoshana Kahn. Today is June 10th, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. It was the 6th of October, and we left the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943. And our-- we arrived in Riga-Kaiserwald. I don't know if it was two days or one day. It seemed to me a long, long time to go. And the train was standing a lot, standing a lot. And we were standing in those-- in that wagon, and that stench, and that hot. There was no air. There was no window. I didn't like the trip at all. Mia was in a terrible position. She was sitting in a puddle of blood. All I was focused on poor Mia Kassel, this lovely lady, she-- lovely girl. She was a few years older than I. And we arrived. And then it-- opened the door. And we-- and it was the most eerie sight one could behold. I mean, totally surrealistic. The light was blue to me, bluish. Everything was bluish. There was-- it was like a-- like an-- there was no greenery. There was no bush. There were people running about in strange outfits, like clowns with blue stripes. There were lots of dogs, lots of SS, and they were barking, the dogs and the SS. They all were barking [GERMAN] fast, fast, [GERMAN]. And then we had to throw all our things in one big heap and line up. It was-- it was like it was bathed in moonlight, but there were kind of big lamps. And it was kind-- it was blue. It was pale. It was blue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what time of day?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a fog. It was night, I thought. It was night. It was blue. Then we were herded in in some barracks. And we all were lying on the floor. And we were separated. And my mom was very upset, because my Aunt Minna was with us then already. And she said I should go and ask for Minna forgiveness for something. I don't know what. She always send me ahead. And then I did say, you know Minna, she want-- Mama wants you to forgive her. She says, OK, I forgive her. [LAUGHS] And then, then the next morning, we had to go to the so-called [GERMAN], I mean, to get clothes. Those days, we did not get uniforms. Only the people who will wear them for a long time, obviously, had the uniform. We were lining up, had to give our clothes and got other clothes. And there was this tall German-Jewish woman-- her name was Gerda Cohen-- and she looked at me and smiled and said, you you shan't-- you shouldn't worry. Girls like you have it easy in a camp. Well, I didn't worry then. And she handed me the most beautiful outfit. It was a jumpsuit, and it had more zippers that-- than you can imagine. Down, and left, and right, oh, it was glorious. It must have come from Germany. It was so beautiful. Now, that was a, a aside. It was-- never seen anything like it. And I had this lovely zippered outfit she handed me. And then we were assigned beds, bunks. And I shared my bunk with my friend, [? Rifka. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your sister? Where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- she was with my mom, and she shared a bed with my mom. And Tanta Minna was there. No, Tanta Minna was already taken away to another-- no. Then they sorted us out. Mia was right away taken out with her bloody, bloody [GERMAN] hanging out, never seen again. Tanta Minna was taken away. Lots of old people got taken away. Roska, Roska and [INAUDIBLE], our neighbors, their little baby Bebutz was already born. She had to-- [INAUDIBLE] said to Roska, look, with a baby, you don't have a chance. Without a baby, you have a chance. Try to get rid of the baby. So Mrs.-- So Roska handed the baby over to an old lady, who had no chance either. And they, they never seen the baby again. It was a beautiful little baby. We called it Bebutz, played with it all the time, her first child. Well, Roska is not doing so good. She's in and out of a mental hospital. Still lives in Tel Aviv and have that in memory, yeah. And, and, and, and Gerda said to me, so girls like you don't have to worry too much. They will have-- have it much easier. And, and we, we moved into that barrack where Gerda was a blockalteste. And she had a sidekick, another German lady. And they had their own little corner. And then we learned very fast of politics. There were-- there was a hierarchy in this camp. First was SS. There were just up there. Nobody touched them. Then came the Germans, the German prisoners, political prisoners and criminals. They were-- they ran the camp. There was X. He was a handsome man. He was-- I understand he was a bank robber before he ended up in there. Gorgeous, looked like Victor Mature, exact spitting image. There was Hannes. Hannes was the one who, who, who was a pimp in his private. That was an [INAUDIBLE]. And he ran the work, the Jews work, blond, his locks and blue-eyed, always making jokes. He used to call us the Easter eggs, Easter bunnies. Oh you're [GERMAN], blah, blah, blah, blah. Then there was Mike. He was Polish. He was a cook. He was political with his son, who was a lovely son, wonderful son, rebels political. Then there was Feldinger, who was a, a, a political prisoner. He ran-- he ran. Then there was, of course, a lageralteste, who was some kind of-- I forgot his name at this point. Then there was a guy who, who ran the, the bookkeeping and the names and all that. And he had a Jewish girl as his secretary with red hair. So those were the, the German prisoners, who were the-- had seniority in the-- in that kibbutz, I almost said, in that camp. They were-- they really ran that camp. You had to be in good footing with them. Afterwards, after that, there were Russian prisoners of war and then there were the Jews. And there were the homosexuals. So they could have been identified. The red triangles, of course, were the communists. The pink triangles were the homosexuals. And the black triangles were the criminals or were the aristocrats. [LAUGHS] And, and the yellow ones were us. So we were on the bottom of the totem pole. And there were we divided those camps. They divided the men, the men camp and [? Frauen ?] camp. And we had--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me. It was-- it was electrical wire and, and those guards sitting on towers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people do you think were in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't know. I don't know. There were-- it was an international camp. There were lots of German Jewish people that were there before. They came from [INAUDIBLE]. They came from Berlin. They came from all over Germany. They were from the Netherlands. They were from Estonia. There were lots of people from Vilna they were there before from-- they were wonderful, those Polish girls. They stuck together and helped each other. They were very good at trading. Oh, they were so clever. And very few Latvians, we were already very few. I mean, there was very few. And next morning, Gerda took me under her big arm. She must have been six foot tall. She wore a big black jacket. And says, come [INAUDIBLE]. We will take a walk along the fence. Maybe somebody will take a liking to you. Well, she told me. I walked down, and nobody took a liking to me. Sex appeal, I didn't have. That's for one. That's one good point on my head. And she said, well, yeah, that was a very good point. But I do remember there was this one Polish lady who was a mistress of the-- Jewish Polish lady was a mistress of the lageralteste. And she lived in our bunk-- right across. So she had a little curtain around her bed. And the other Polish lady were like help-- handing her and bringing her and what. She was sitting there with the red cheeks. And she got all the presents. It was amazing. Then there was a German girl who was X's girlfriend. She got special treatment. There was a Viennese girl who was Hannes' girlfriend. And she and her sister got special treatments. Her sister name was Gerda. They came from Vienna [INAUDIBLE]. And I said, one day I see Gerda, and she wears my little medal [INAUDIBLE] Uncle David sent me once from Africa. It says, to my dear [INAUDIBLE]-- that's how my nickname from Uncle David with a little Magen David and a diamond. I said, Gerda, this is my-- and she said, nein, it's Gerda's [GERMAN]. Hannes' hand this [GERMAN]. Now it's mine and the Hannes' belongs to me. But I had my book. I had-- there somehow, I got my books, by Stefan Zweig. And I read that. Yeah, life in the concentration camp was very interesting once you got used to the tempo and the rhythm of things, what you should do, what you cannot do, what is good for you, and what-- you learn that very fast. For example, when you were on appell or selections--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early in the morning, we had to get out fast, fast from the things and, and line up and were counted by an SS person and by Gerda. And you, you better stand next to a good-looking person, not to a Muselmann. You know the expression Muselmann? You had better not stand next to a Muselmann. Because what can happen, the Muselmann is taken, and you get-- you get with him, go with him somehow. Get next to a good-looking person, next to a young person, next to a strong person. And be on your own. If you are loyal, you have less chances. I learned that very fast. And then we were assigned jobs. I worked on the [GERMAN], on the [GERMAN], on the airport. We were building something. I mean, we were took the lorry going, took it back, whatever. And, and I don't know what we did, highways. And Feldinger was our, our-- Feldinger, that German guy was the one who was responsible for us. He had a little hut. There he sat. And there, a, a, a girl made for him cup of coffee. To work with that, in that part, was a very good thing, didn't have to be outside. So I-- we went every morning out there and sang, going there and coming home, and sang.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: All sorts of things. [SINGING IN GERMAN] All those stupid things they told us to sing. I mean, they're supposed to be cheerful. And it's supposed to be-- do that job.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, what we ate was-- living on the rations of the camp was impossible. That was already a given. You just died. And anybody who had no [? outdoor ?]. My mom and my sister stayed inside. They did something with batteries, the little children. And my mom was in, in camps. They didn't go to [INAUDIBLE] The people who went to [INAUDIBLE] had a better chance to get something. Living on the rations was, was a death knell. So you had to find somebody and we-- there was something always to be found. I do remember on the road to work was always a piece of bread next to a stone. That was one thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then there was an old man who had a big-- kept on the airport, he had a big [? spires ?] there, or [INAUDIBLE], Latvian man. And I had this thing, a, a code with him. I used to come there, good morning. Can I have some paper? And then he handed me a big piece of paper, and there was a sandwich in it, OK? And this was great. That was great. And sometimes, we exchanged things for potatoes. That was lovely. And once, once I found a whole fresh baked bread in the-- hidden in a stove. Now, I was always raised to be honest. It's not my bread, not supposed to take it. Whose bread is it? And then something told me, you had better take it. That was a feast. I took that bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I usually-- my little sister used to at some-- at some time, my little sister used to come along when they stop that. And we used to put things in our bra, so she should have a little bust and put stuff in her shoes, so she'd be a little taller, so she'd look bigger. And I didn't stand next to her because then I would be the difference. And I used to have her carry things. I thought a child would, you know, again. But it didn't work. We had a very gruesome SS lady called Koba. And she was really cruel. And she used to call everybody Sara. Everyone, every Jewish girl was Sara, and every man was Abraham. I don't know something. We all Saras. Our name could have been Hildegaard. We were Saras.

speaker: interviewer
text: And said, hey Sara, and so on. And they took the potatoes from the child. I can never forget that. And I feel so guilty because why should I-- did I have her carry it? I thought it would be safer with her, which was a stupid thing. I did what my mom did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one very, very unpleasant memory I have, because we had to take turns in ladling out the, the so-called dinner soup. Sometimes, one-- not all of us turns. We had too many. But sometimes, somebody was appointed, and I was appointed to ladle out the soup. Now, I had the power over the big cauldron.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know Kathe Kollwitz, the painter? Very famous, she did paintings after World War I. And she was a, a socialist, and she always had paintings about-- of children who are hungry, or mothers what are sad, or thing. She has one painting, which is called 'Hunger,' lots of faces with little eyes looking up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here comes the day I have to ladle out the soup. Every bit on your head and order. One spoon from the top, and one spoon from the bottom. The bottom, there were some rotten potatoes, some lentils, and some horse meat, very little. Now, I had a chance to give my mother two spoons from the bottom. And they're coming closer. My mom comes closer. And all the eyes, like in the Kathe Kollwitz picture look at me and watch me. And I was a coward. I gave one ladle from the top and one ladle from the bottom. And my mother broke into tears.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had this chance. I didn't dare. I thought they are going to hang-- bite me, all of them. They were so hungry. I should have said, you know what? Who the hell-- well, today, in hindsight, I should have said, it's my mom, after all. What do you want me to do? I didn't have the guts. OK? Bad, that's a bad memory, how she cried. Shouldn't have cried, at least. Should have understood that I didn't have the guts, didn't have the guts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then people found protectors. And high and below, I found a protector in that camp. His name was [? Jacob, ?] and he was a political prisoner and had spent most of his life in prisons as a communist. He was ancient, 45 years old, ancient, and bent over. I remember him well, had a red complexion, was bald, had yellow teeth and had a way to laugh at everything, and was extremely educated, because all the years he spent in prison, he used to eat up libraries.

speaker: interviewer
text: So yeah, there were times in the beginning that on Sundays, when the people from the camp could cross over, the men, the husbands, and so on, and on, strange things happened on the-- in the barracks. And yeah, and one day, I see [? Jacob ?] coming over. He wore that striped uniform, and had this strange bosom, a bosom, was this little man with bosom. He had sandwiches under those things, which I right away smelled like a dog. I stood there and stared at him. So he said, would you like a-- would you like a sandwich. I said, oh, yeah. And then he gave me the sandwich. That's the whole friendship started.

speaker: interviewee
text: He taught me astrology, math, history. I mean, he was a walking encyclopedia, this lovely old man. And he was a communist. And he always said to me, I can't understand why the Jews are always [GERMAN], you know, bending with the SS. Why don't they just step on their toes that nobody sees, and all that big deal. You know, he was German. And I don't-- and then he said, besides, I said, the Jews are all our enemies. He was antisemitic on top of it. Enemies, that means against the [INAUDIBLE] rich people, the bourgeoisie. And I'm coming the working class, and so on and on. But he took care of [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] was me. [GERMAN] means somebody whose nose is always running. Since [GERMAN] had a protector, I was saved from X, and, and, and, and Hannes, and the whole lot, where lots of girls had a lot of trouble with. Or maybe it wasn't trouble. But I tell you what, it was always better than to die, whatever they did, although it was not very-- I can imagine, not pleasant. It is good looking girls, you know, whatever. There were in the camp, there's those little boys who had to take care of the-- of the SS and of the [GERMAN] of the Germans. So they're are used for all sorts of purposes, not only to-- to clean those boots, for other ways too. They were abused sexually.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get those stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that we know all that. We lived there a year. We lived there a year. That was-- that always was the politics of the camp. And then [? Jacob ?] told me always everything. I mean, [? Jacob-- ?] [? Jacob ?] always warned us on certain things. He was great in that respect. He wasn't great in many respects. He got shot whenever he tried to flee. He was an old man already. By the time he was 46, he was very old. [LAUGHS] Poor [? Jacob. ?] Anyhow, for example, in the Riga ghetto-- was an entity in itself. And there was a Jew called Einstein, relative of the famous Einstein. And he was a big shot in the Riga ghetto. He was very close to the-- to the SS something or other. And he came with his so-called papers to the Kaiserwald for special treatment from-- but he didn't know that Kaiserwald is not Riga ghetto. There are different kings, and different queens, and different, different politics. What works in the Riga ghetto does not necessarily. I just see him one day walking with a lageralteste, and this SS man dressed up and shiny things. Christmas, [? Jacob ?] told me, you wait what's going to happen. They took Einstein, the S-- X and Hannes and the lot, and drowned him in the toilet head first, you know, if the latrine, in the [? caca. ?] That what happened to Einstein. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He should have been smarter than that and know that is better for him to be in the shadows and not to try out, outshine X and Hannes. That what they needed. They were very cruel. X was an especial cruel man. He was Victor Mature and a streak of cruelty. And, and, and once he caught me reading that book, and he said to me, [INAUDIBLE], you know what? I will test you on that book. And if you make one mistake, this is the end of you. I read it very fast. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What else does one want to know about a, a-- concentration camp politics?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, of course, there was the selections. And I had a girlfriend called Helen Lurgenson. She came from the Netherlands. She was religious. That I remember. She knew how to pray. And she was already abused by the Dutch SS. Her, her, her, her chest was all cut up. And she had a heart-- a heart disease, probably as a result of abuse. She had heart disease. But everybody liked Helen. Our doctor in camp was Dr. [? Dalek. ?] He was a Polish prisoner, and he was a fine man. And then was a, a, a Jewish doctor. At this point I don't know a name-- from Vilna. And we had this little hospital there. And Helen very often was in that hospital. And they work-- and they used to take out the sick and take them back to Riga [GERMAN] where they got executed, the sick ones. They always tried to hide Helen under the-- Helena under the blanket, so nobody should see her. She really liked Helen. She was such a lovely child. And we always used to stand hand-in-hand together and make fun about, I don't know about Mickey Rooney and Freddie. But I knew that was my favorite one. He played Fauntleroy. And then you are going to boyfriend's going to Mickey. And mines going to Freddie. And then we exchanged.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is he doing now in America? And what is he eating for breakfast? Those are having him to talking about us being killed. And we really got-- so when it was-- when we were finished playing, it was over. And we had a-- that's how we whiled the horrible, horrible time away. And she prayed a lot. She was religious. One day, she said to me, you had better not hold hands with me today. I says, why? She says, you'll see. Stay away from me. And I did. And they took her. She knew. She wanted my comb. I had a broken comb. She combed her hair. You know, they took her. And I went back to the bunker, and I had my soup, because that was a normal thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The big selection was one wintry morning. I don't know when. And they had a special doctor come. He was-- and somewhere in a book I have his name. But it's not important. He was handsome, elegant. They must have had a fabulous night, because all the-- there were lots of German prostitutes that had houses themselves. Very-- well, they were wild. And they were very busy there. Hannes looked good. They, the lageralteste, the SS all looked good, freshly shaven. Their faces were, were, were red. They had probably to drink and all that. And they had a little stage. And there was the doctor. And there was Hannes. And there was X and the lageralteste. And the doctor, the SS man had a little stick like laying on a bandstand [INAUDIBLE]. And we had to line up and walk in the dark, you know, walk that huddle and in front of him. And I did right, and to the left, right and to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you dressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you dressed at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah we had our uniforms. At that time, we had our uniforms already. I, I don't think so. We had already-- they took away my beautiful jumpsuit, and I had a striped thing. Our hair was already, not shaven yet. I don't know when they shaved the head at this point. They shaved the head. And my mom was hanging on with me on the back, and my little sister. And we were walking. I saw that a girl from my class did not want to leave her mother. And she went to the-- to the side where I didn't like the look of the people. You know, you had-- you know where you should be. And he and I moved up. And this doctor looked at me. And he sees Adolf's sweetest dream, blonde, tall, light complexion. I mean, they couldn't have a better example of Aryan womanhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said to me, you aren't Jewish or are you? And I said, what do you think, Herr [GERMAN] blah, blah, blah, fuhrer. I've been here for whole long [GERMAN] Kaiswerwald. I'm here on holiday, on vacation in the the beautiful Kaiserwald. And he said-- he laughed. And Hannes [GERMAN], Hannes said, oh, she just looks like that. A big help, him, yeah, looks like that. [INAUDIBLE] She just looks like that. Of course, I look like that. But that was a whole idea of the-- of the theory. I just look like that. And he sent me over with mom and the kid to the good side, of course. And everybody was shouting, and why are they all laughing? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were a year in that camp. We made it through a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- there were Russian prisoners of war there, too. And when we came back from work, the truck with the prisoners of war were standing at the gate and waiting us. And there was that guy who was a lieutenant or whatever. His name was Kersher. He was maybe Jewish. He looked it. He had dark eyes, dark hair, very handsome. And he gave an order. And all those poor soldiers had to hand us their rations. They were ghastly. They were sour. They were made of a-- but they handed it to us. We took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which soldiers were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're Russian prisoners of war. They had to give us their rations. He told them to. And one day, said in Russian, [INAUDIBLE], he said, my girls, I'm sorry to tell you, we are leaving. And you'll have to fend on yourself, for yourself. And he was crying. You know what happened to those prisoners of war. They went back to Russian and Stalin executed them all. But I didn't know it then. But every day, those Russians were giving me, us, all of us, their breads, all the rations, whatever they had. He saw that everybody hands it to the-- to the girls, talking about human rights. I have a soft spot for Russians, by the way, another one of my beloved people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are we? In, in, in Kaiserwald. We were there a year. We managed, more or less. From time to time, they, they took the sick out from the hospital and took them in the-- in the trucks. The truck had the gas. They turned on the gas in there, and they came to, to a place, they were already dead. How did you find out about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Jacob. ?] Everybody knew about it. I remember standing there and those Muselmanns were walking, you know, bloated. And they said, you know where we are going. And then came the day where they collected all the little children. Not my sister, though. I don't know why. She wasn't there. But all the little children, hand-in-hand like going to kindergarten. The mothers walked them to that green truck. Koba was yelling, fast, fast, [SPEAKING GERMAN] you know, Sara, Sara. And they herded those lovely little children into that truck, like a kindergarten, hand-in-hand, as if they go to the zoo today. When I see kids going hand-in-hand, I see those visions. I see one little girl, especially. She had her head down. She was walking and had little blonde curls in the back to the neck.

speaker: interviewer
text: The mothers waved goodbye, turned around, went back to the barracks, and the kids were [INAUDIBLE]. The children were the most horrendous memory of mine. That is-- I could manage anything. But if I think of the children, that is-- their helplessness, and they trusted us. And because-- my little sister used to ask, [INAUDIBLE], is [GERMAN]? You what that means? Pick kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they pick only kids. I mean, when my granddaughter says, Bubbe, will we go tomorrow to the zoo? Her life was is-- is [GERMAN] Kinder Action? I said, no, Miriam. [SPEAKING GERMAN] I mean, this is her life, Kinder Action or not Kinder Action. And she knew what's happening to her. You don't come back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean anything, but that is beyond forgiveness. That is that the helpless little souls, Innocent like what? And nobody could help them, nobody. There was [? Mashinka ?] in our-- in our camp in the beginning, a little boy from Vilna with his mom. And those Vilna girls were absolutely great. They stuck together, and they helped each other. They were loyal. They were courageous. They were a real fabulous lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this-- the mother was young, had long hair. And she-- and they used to hide [? Mashinka. ?] When they were looking for kids, they used to give him something to drink and hide him, and lift up something under the floors and [? Mashinka. ?] And one day they got [? Mashinka. ?] They got him. He was a-- I mean, everybody played with him. He was the doll. One day they got [? Mashinka, ?] and they got him. And they tried-- the girls tried very much to save the mother. She was screaming. So all the-- well, she was exactly opposite my bunk. I was sitting on my bunk and see the whole scenario. She was screaming.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]
